questions.question: What is χ(G), the chromatic number of a graph?
options: The smallest k such that G has a proper k-coloring
The number of vertices with maximum degree
questions.explanation: χ(G) is the minimum number of colors needed so that no edge connects two same-colored vertices. Computing χ is NP-hard in general; bounded above by Δ + 1 (Brooks' theorem), and exactly 2 for bipartite graphs.

questions.question: A graph is bipartite iff:
options: It has no odd cycles (equivalently, χ ≤ 2; vertices partition into two color classes with all edges between them)
questions.explanation: Bipartite ⇔ 2-colorable ⇔ no odd cycle. The BFS test colors alternately along a tree; conflicts (a non-tree edge connecting two same-color vertices) reveal an odd cycle. Crucial: many CS structures (e.g., interns-projects matching) live in bipartite graphs.

questions.question: Why is bipartite max-matching polynomial while general (non-bipartite) max-matching needs Edmonds' blossom algorithm?
options: Augmenting paths in bipartite graphs are well-behaved (alternating edges always work). In non-bipartite graphs, odd cycles ('blossoms') break naive augmenting-path search; Edmonds shrinks them and recurses.
questions.explanation: Augmenting paths in bipartite graphs alternate side-A and side-B vertices naturally. Non-bipartite graphs can have odd cycles where the alternation fails. Edmonds' breakthrough was the 'blossom contraction' trick — shrink the odd cycle, find an augmenting path in the contracted graph, then expand. Bipartite avoids that entirely → Kuhn / Hopcroft-Karp are linear-time elegant.

questions.question: Euler's formula for a connected planar graph states:
questions.explanation: V - E + F = 2 for any planar embedding of a connected graph (counting one outer face). Beautiful constraint: lets you prove K₅ and K₃,₃ are non-planar by deriving impossible F counts. Generalizes to surfaces via Euler characteristic.

questions.question: Greedy coloring with the wrong vertex order on a 'bipartite double star' can use how many colors?
options: Up to (1/2) · n in the worst case — the bad ordering pairs each side's vertex with the previously-colored side's vertex sequentially, forcing one new color per step
questions.explanation: Greedy coloring is O(V + E) but its quality depends on vertex order. Best ordering achieves χ; worst can be much worse — on a bipartite graph (χ = 2), pathological orderings have been constructed to require Ω(n) colors. In practice, ordering vertices by descending degree (Welsh-Powell) works well.

questions.question: How is register allocation in a compiler reduced to graph coloring?
options: Variables are vertices; an edge connects two variables that are 'live' at the same program point (so they can't share a register). A k-coloring corresponds to a register assignment using k registers; we want χ ≤ available_registers.
questions.explanation: The interference graph: vertex per variable, edge for 'simultaneously live.' χ(interference) ≤ k means k registers suffice for that function. When χ > k, the compiler must spill — store some variable to memory. Phase 08 Lesson 16 implements this with Chaitin-Briggs heuristic.
